State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission

P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance/Administration . . . Executive/Legal . . . Enforcement/Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5660                          322-5901                322-6441

March 7, 1977


Mr. John F. Powell, Counsel
Bay Area Air Pollution Control
District
939 Ellis St.
San Francisco, Calif. 94109

Dear Mr. Powell:

     Thank you for your letter of March 2, 1977. Please be advised
that the Fair Political Practices Commission will include Advisory
Board members in the Conflict of Interest Code of the Bay Area
Pollution Control District only if the members of the Advisory Board
satisfy the requirements for being a public official within 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (1).

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr.
                                        Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr., Chief
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division
                                        (916) 322-6444

DIS:mfn

BAY AREA AIR POLLUTION CONTROL DISTRICT

MAR 4 § 23 Mil March 2, 1977

Re: Conflict of Interest Code for
the Bay Area Air Pollution
Control District

ALAMEDA COUNTY
Fred E Cooper
Richard Oliver
Charles Santana
Helen Tiraski

CONTRA COSTA COUNTY
James P. Kenny
Lindon Lance

MARIN COUNTY
Stuart C. Culler

NAPA COUNTY
Sam Chapman
(Vice Chairperson)

N FRANCISCO COUNTY
Arthur P. Gould
Jim Robertson
Peter Tarasdi

SAN MATEO COUNTY
Edward J. Radenski, Jr
Virginia Logue

ANTA CLARA COUNTY
Hugh R. Busch, Jr.
John Howell
Daniel (Jack) Mello
(Secretary)
Geraldine R Shoemake

SOLANO COUNTY
James Lemor

SONOMA COUNTY
Frank N Pigguebach

State of California
Fair Political Practices
Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Commission Members:

On February 9, 1977 Mr. D. J. Callaghan transmitted to your
Commission the proposed Conflict of Interest Code adopted by
the District's Board of Directors on January 19, 1977.
On February 10, 1977 I wrote your Commission concerning the
proposed Code. Both letters detailed the concern raised at
the Board's hearing relative to the inclusion of the Advisory
Council as designated employees.

At the February 16, 1977 meeting of the District's Board of
Directors Mrs. Ruth Church Gupta, chairperson of the Advisory
Council, addressed the Board with respect to the Board's
inclusion of the members of the Advisory Council as "designated
employees." Mrs. Gupta reviewed for the Board the report
"Action Taken by Advisory Council Executive Committee with
Respect to Inclusion in Conflict of Interest Code Adopted by
the Board of Directors January 19, 1977" which urged the
Board to withdraw the inclusion of the Advisory Council
from the Conflict of Interest Code. A copy of this report
is attached.

Mrs. Gupta also reviewed various aspects of the law relating
to "designated employees" with the Board and discussed advice
recently given to the State Board of Control by the legal
counsel to State Controller Cory. She indicated that the
counsel to the Controller had expressed the feeling that
Advisory Council members should not be included as "designated
employees." She also reviewed the statutory provision which
requires that members of the Advisory Council must come from
specific disciplines.

In its discussion of this matter, the Board adopted a resolution
asking me to meet with the Advisory Council to discuss with
them the possibility of amending the Code to limit the dis-
closure made by Council members. Members of the Council would
be put in a category such that less than all financial interests
would have to be reported. It was suggested that they might
be limited in their disclosure to interests in regulated
industries.

939 ELLIS STREET • SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94109 • (415) 771-6000

State of California
Fair Political Practices
Commission

March 2, 1977
Page 2

I will, of course, be available to meet with the Advisory Council
to discuss possible modifications of the scope of disclosure required
of members of the Council. Such a question of possible modification,
however, will be an issue for consideration by the Board of Directors only in the event that your Commission concludes that members
of the Advisory Council are properly determined to be "designated
employees."

Pertinent portions of the minutes of the Board of Directors
meeting of February 16, 1977 are enclosed.

Please advise if I may be of any assistance in this matter.

Very truly yours,

John F. Powell
Counsel

JFP:vld
Enclosures

cc: Alfred Nelder
Ruth Gupta
D. J. Callaghan

February 4,1977

ACTION TAKEN BY ADVISORY COUNCIL EXECUTIVE COMMITTEE
WITH RESPECT TO INCLUSION IN CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE ADOPTED BY THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS
JANUARY 19, 1977

The Executive Committee to submit to the Board of Directors
the following report with respect to the Board's adopted
Conflict of Interest Code:

That the Executive Committee takes exception to the
Board's inclusion of the Advisory Council for the
following reasons:

(1) It is clear that it was not the intent for advisory
Boards, who are not compensated and not employees,
to be included under the provisions of the Code,
as per §32019 of the Government Code, as follows:

"Designated employee" does not include an
elected state officer or any unsalaried member
of any board or commission which serves a solely advisory function."

(2) That it appears to be in direct conflict with the
enabling legislation setting up the Advisory Council;

(3) That such an inclusion was decided without a
hearing of the Advisory Council;

and furthermore,

That the Executive Committee understands from the
Minutes of the Board meeting that the motivation for
including the Advisory Council is for the purpose of
improving the image of the District. If the latter
is correct, it seems that it would be appropriate to
refer such a matter to the Advisory Council for
suggestions as to how the image of the District might
be improved and comment on any specific ideas that the
Board may be contemplating in this respect;

That the Executive Committee would like to point out
the inaccuracy in the Board Minutes of January 19th
that Advisory Council members were advised during their
personal interview of the Board's intention to act with
respect to conflict of interest or disclosure;

That the point that "since many of its (Council's)
recommendations are adopted by the Board" the Council
does in effect make decisions is invalid. According to
this view, the only Advisory Council referred to in the
echoes of FPPO would be an advisory body whose
recommendations are not in anyway adopted.

Therefore, the Committee urges the Board to withdraw
the inclusion of the Advisory Council from the Code.




mh

2.

Chairperson Nelder called the meeting to order at 10:15 A.M.

APPROVAL OF MINUTES OF FEBRUARY 2, 1977

On motion of Mr. Tamaras; seconded by Mr. Kenny; carried.

REPORT OF THE ADVISORY COUNCIL - CONFLICT OF INTEREST CODE
Ruth Church Gupta, Chairperson

Mrs. Gupta reviewed the report, Action Taken by Advisory Council
Executive Committee with Respect to Inclusion in Conflict of Interest
Code adopted by the Board of Directors February 4, 1977 urging the
Board to withdraw the inclusion of the Advisory Council from the
Code. She advised that the Executive Committee did discuss the
disqualification portion of the Code with respect to service on the
Council, pointing out that the law specifically requires Council
members to be from specific disciplines; that the legal counsel
to State Controller Cory advised the State Board of Control that
the FPPC intends to be more exclusive than inclusive in the Designated
Employee area and that it was his feeling that Advisory Council
members are not included in said designation; that the Executive
Committee did poll the members inquiring about the question of
conflict of interest being asked when interviewed, and that although
some members could not recall, the feeling generally was that they
were asked whether they were involved in any activity which might
create a conflict of interest to which the response was that they
saw no problem; that the Executive Committee did discuss the possi-
bility of adopting a program that would state the Council's philosophy
that anyone having a conflict would disclose it without going into
the reporting requirements of a lot of paperwork which would not
accomplish the purpose of the legislation.

In discussion, Mrs. Gupta stated that the Council members were
polled specifically as to the following questions:

Was the subject of conflict of interest raised, and an answer
requested;

Was a willingness to disclose asked, and an answer given,

during their interview with the Personnel Committee and the answers
generally were "no".

Mrs. Koehler advised that she had requested Ms. Harder to check the
tape of the Personnel Committee meeting of November 17, 1976, when
the list of questions asked of the Applicants reviewed and
conflict of interest was not among them. She stated that during
the interviews the Personnel Committee became aware that many
consultants had applied to be on the Council, and that the question
of conflict of interest was asked of some of the applicants because
the Committee was concerned they wanted to serve for their own gain,
but that the question was not asked of all Advisory Council candidates.
She apologized for any confusion in this area.

- 2 -

Mr. Cooper stated that Alameda County Counsel advised that it
might be possible to adopt a Code requiring filing of income and
investments in regulated industries and suggested the Council
discuss this approach with the Legal Counsel.

After further discussion, Mr. Cooper moved to instruct the Counsel
to meet with the Advisory Council in an attempt to develop a more
restrictive list of what particular interests need to be divulged
by members of the advisory council and report back to the Board
as to how it might restrict it to those areas where there are
conflicts; seconded by Mr. Tamaras.

In discussion, Mr. Bacciocco pointed out that the Board wanted to
make sure that anyone serving on the Council did not profit indirect
or directly by a collective relationship with a source which appears
before the Board and that if the Council keeps that in mind every
member of the public will be protected.

Mr. Chapman stated that he continued to support the concept of
disclosure by the Advisory Council because it makes recommendations
which are often adopted by the Board in total but that he was willing
to narrow the approach to a more reasonable approach of disclosure.

Mr. Cooper stated that perhaps the Advisory Council should be
excluded completely, pointing out that these were people who were
volunteering their time and it is an unnecessary burden upon them;
that the Board was not necessarily persuaded by the opinions of the
Council since it had the Staff input also; that he was against the
entire concept.

On inquiry from Mrs. Koehler, Mr. Powell stated that when submitting
the Code to the FPPC, he reviewed the background with respect to
the issue of Designated Employee and he read from his letter dated
February 10, 1977, in which he advised that the Board expected the
Commission to review and pass on the matter. Mr. Powell pointed out
that BCDC did not include its Citizens Advisory Body in its Code;
that the San Francisco Art Commission is not included; that the
opinion of some City Attorneys is that an advisory body should not be
included; that the Los Angeles City Council has been asked to
reevaluate their Designated Employees; that the FPPC would probably
hold a hearing on the Code adopted by the Board; that if the motion
passes he would send another letter to the FPPC with respect to the
action taken.

On inquiry from Mr. Santana, Mr. Powell stated that amendments to
the Code could be made because of changed circumstances but that he
did not feel a hearing needed to be held on the amendments and that
the Board could at any time take action to amend the Code which would
be sent to the FPPC for approval.

Mr. Santana moved a substitute motion to exclude the Advisory Council
from the Conflict of Interest Code; there was no second.

- 3 -

Mr. Cooper's motion carried on roll call:

AYES:    BACCIOCCO, CHAPMAN, COOPER, CULLEN, DOETSCH,
         KENNY, KOHLIER, LANCE, LEMOS, McCORQUODALE,
         NELDER, OLIVER, POZNAHOVICII, SANTANA, STEINBERG,
         TIRSELL.

NOES:    NONE.

ABSENT:  LEIPZIG, TAMAPAS.

REPORT OF THE ENVIRONMENTAL MANAGEMENT TASK FORCE
         Sam Chapman, Vice Chairperson

Mr. Chapman briefly reviewed the report, Schedule of Issues for
Presentation before the Environmental Management Task Force,
dated February 1, 1977. He advised that the last meeting of the
EMTF was concerned with surface water runoff.

DISCUSSION ON NEW SOURCE REVIEW AND EPA'S SUGGESTED OFF-SET
POLICY

Mr. Callaghan reviewed his report, Emission "Trade-off" or "Off-set",
dated February 15, 1977. He advised that EPA was holding a hearing
in San Francisco on February 23, 1977 with respect to the matter
and requested guidance from the Board for a possible preliminary
draft on a trade-off regulation.

Mr. Powell reviewed his letter dated January 31, 1977 re: EPA
"Trade-off" Policy.

On inquiry from Mr. Kenny, Mr. Powell advised that EPA has a
trade-off policy which is effective in 6 states; that EPA has
approved a plan with respect to New Source Review for the District
but not throughout the entire state; that EPA's present requirement
is that every state have procedures whereby new construction will be
prohibited if it interferes with the attainment or maintenance of
air quality standards; that EPA may make a change in its basic
regulation.

Mr. Cooper stated that it was clear that EPA will allow the District
to adopt a trade-off policy and he urged that the District so do,
pointing out that if this had been in effect when Dow made its application they would have been able to build in the District rather than
in a foreign country; that there was an inherent unfairness in allowing
a plant already in the area to have a trade-off but not allow a new
plant to be built; that the adoption of the trade-off policy will
allow new industry and new jobs to locate in the Bay Area.

He suggested the following: (1) that the
Board instruct Staff to work with the Advisory Council on preparing
a draft new rule; (2) that the Board instruct Mr. Chapman to indicate

- 4 -

sections.conclusion: Please be advised that the Fair Political Practices Commission will include Advisory Board members in the Conflict of Interest Code of the Bay Area Pollution Control District only if the members of the Advisory Board satisfy the requirements for being a public official within 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (1).

sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District's Advisory Council should be included as 'designated employees' under the District's Conflict of Interest Code, requiring financial disclosure.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC will only include Advisory Board members in the Conflict of Interest Code if they meet the statutory definition of 'public official' under California Administrative Code Section 18700(a)(1).
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: This is a short advisory response from FPPC Chief Delbert L. Spurlock, Jr. The question is inferred from the incoming letter from counsel John F. Powell and supporting documents (Advisory Council report and Board meeting minutes). No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections are explicitly labeled, but the substance is clear from context.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether members of the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District's Advisory Council should be included as 'designated employees' under the District's Conflict of Interest Code, requiring financial disclosure.

CONCLUSION: Please be advised that the Fair Political Practices Commission will include Advisory Board members in the Conflict of Interest Code of the Bay Area Pollution Control District only if the members of the Advisory Board satisfy the requirements for being a public official within 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a) (1).

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to a request regarding whether Advisory Council members of the Bay Area Air Pollution Control District should be included as 'designated employees' subject to financial disclosure requirements under the District's Conflict of Interest Code. The Commission conditionally stated it would include them only if they satisfy the statutory requirements for being a public official.
